posts.name: Erlend @ntonsen
posts.cooked: I just periodically check in on the weather forcasts for a couple of villages, I have no inside information, so I was hoping that someone could chime in on how things are looking so far? Seems like there wasnt much spring frost and so far no huge heat spikes, although looks like there has been more rain these past few weeks than usual?

posts.name: Edvards Antonovs
posts.cooked:
No clue how good it was considered up until yesterday but looks like some serious hail hit Meursault and perhaps some other villages with localised heavy damage to the fruit.

Thibaud Clerget has some stories on instagram if anyone uses that channel of time-wasting.

Edit: some more information here.

posts.name: Nikolaj Lehto
posts.cooked:
Meursault and surrounding villages were hit hard… Reports and pictures coming in of golf ball-sized hail and some very destroyed grape bunches.

Winehog - with a passion – 12 Jul 23

Hailstorms over Burgundy

The morning sun has revealed all the carnage caused by the violent thunderstorm last night, July 11. As always, it will take several days to fully

Est. reading time: 3 minutes

posts.name: Craig Gleason
posts.cooked:
N.Lehto:

Reports and pictures coming in of golf ball-sized hail

I feel it was a mistake for Europe to adopt the larger American golf ball in 1990. These hailstorms are worse for it now.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: The hail storm in question (there were others in the Côte de Nuits) started in Rochepot, came down the combe of Saint Aubin, then veered into Meursault before heading into the plain. There are parts of Meursault, especially what one might call south-central Meursault, with a reasonable amount of damage, maybe up to 30% in places. Parts of Saint-Aubin also suffered. But it’s not as bad as one might have feared, the vines will bounce back, and dry, warm and windy weather now will help dry out the impacted berries. My parcel in southern Beaune, for example, was hit to the extent of perhaps 1-3% - which the 35mm of water we got with the hail will largely compensate for.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
William_Kelley:

William,

When I was in Burgundy in early June, producers I spoke with were concerned about a lack of rain at that time. Did this storm (or other storms) help with regard to that more generally in Burgundy?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Nothing is ever perfect in farming, but so far this year we have plenty of water in Burgundy. We’re getting just enough just when we need it. Preferably, however, it won’t be delivered “on the rocks”.

posts.name: Rick Allen
posts.cooked: When is harvest expected this year? I’m going to be in Beaune in early/mid October for a week, and was hoping that things would have started to slow down by then…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Bulk of the action should be 5-20 Sept at the time of writing.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: We got another 25 mm in Beaune last night… definitely looking good with regards to water at this stage!

posts.name: Edvards Antonovs
posts.cooked:
Some major weather events are forecasted for Monday-Wednesday in South Europe moving into more Central parts.

Let’s hope France gets spared but it looks like Bordeaux might be on the edge if the graphic is to be believed.

More here.

posts.name: Mich@el Ch@ng
posts.cooked:
EdvardsA:

Generally or in burgundy specifically? I don’t think burgundy is in need of cooling down this season, no? At least looking at it for the past couple of weeks the temperature seems to have stayed within 24-28 degrees Celsius which is one of the cooler years in recent history.

I meant Europe in general.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Yeah, we just had another 15mm or so of rain today, with temps in the mid-20s during the day for the rest of the week.

If we can get through to the finish line without succumbing to the various disease pressures, we could have something rather special on our hands.

posts.name: Erlend @ntonsen
posts.cooked: Any updates? Seems like there’s been no shortage of rain…

posts.name: Phil T r o t t i e r
posts.cooked: Mildiou seems to have hit the Bordelais quite seriously this year but I’ve read that it looked like Burgundy wasn’t under the same duress and would be able to handle it properly. Anybody has more insight into this?
posts.display_username: Phil T r o t t i e r

posts.name: Edvards Antonovs
posts.cooked:
Yeah, lots of rain but temperatures are still nice from what I’m seeing. Next week it’s going to be very sunny and 30+ degrees, nice boost. But so far haven’t seen anyone say at what stage the grapes are at and should we hope for temperate conditions or full-on sun blasting.

The few growers I follow are setting their harvest times between 13 of September to 20ish.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
We are at late véraison at this stage.

The summer has been temperate and on the wet side thus far, but for the last few days we have been hitting the low 30s Centigrade, and that is set to continue through early next week before things cool off.

Some rain is forecast over the next couple of days, and how much we get will be critical: too much, and berries may burst, opening the door to botrytis. But, harvest will be getting started in just over three weeks, so there is not long to go now!

Tasting a few berries here and there, what is remarkable is that, at least in well farmed parcels, there is already next to zero in terms of herbaceous flavors, on the contrary, there is already sweet fruit, and the pips already show signs of coloring up. In 2022, at the same stage, everything was blocked due to lack of water and the fruit was very pulpy with green pips.

posts.name: bruce curfman
posts.cooked:
Any update from feet on the ground?
From my far-away forecast it appears that the heat spike is over and cooler weather ahead. With continued chances of rain off and on the next couple of weeks I was wondering if harvest forecasts have been pushed back significantly.

(We’ll be there beginning the 5th.)

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Hi Bruce!

We had less rain than forecast today: 10mm vs the predicted 25mm. Since then, the sky has opened and a nice north wind has established itself. What’s more, the forecasted rain events on 5-6 Sept have now evaporated. All of this takes us away from a sort of 1994 scenario (lots of rain on ripe grapes) and in a more interesting direction: maybe something like 1983 or 2001 in white, and a sort of 1993-2002-2017 hybrid in red. Clearly, the heat wave we just endured will have changed the profile of the wines; I could see that happening in real time, tasting the grapes. But now we’re back to cooler weather, and if it stays dryish it could really be very exciting. The grapes are fragile, after water, heat and then water again; and PN is more fragile than Chardonnay at this stage. Seeds are already much more brown than they were at this stage last year, yet this year people will likely pick a bit later. PYCM starts on Monday I think, Olivier Lamy on Sept 2; Lalou picked d’Auvenay Criots and Chevalier yesterday, afraid of heavy rain and hail. I’m likely going to pick my barrel of old vine Meursault on 3 or 4 Sept, and am agonizing about my Beaune 1er Cru parcel. So, it’s the sort of year where tasting a lot of grapes and having a flexible picking team is going to make a big difference!

posts.name: bruce curfman
posts.cooked:
Thank you so much for the update, William! Obviously, if some of the big names are already harvesting Chardonnay, things haven’t been pushed back much, if at all.

Good to hear of the improvement in the forecast. Fingers crossed!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Just ran into JF Coche at the Beaune market this morning. He sees a strong 1983 analogy in white. They’ll start picking on the 2nd.

posts.name: Erlend @ntonsen
posts.cooked: Thanks for the updates! Very interesting to follow.

posts.name: Charlie Carnes
posts.cooked: Agree, love getting these updates on the weather from someone there! Cool to read.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: I remember the 1983 reds, a very mixed bag from really good to really bad. But, I don’t remember what the 1983 whites were like. Help?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Ripe, concentrated, good tension, with sometimes a touch of botrytis/sur maturité for people who picked on the late side. JFC related how he picked a week earlier than everyone in Meursault and came in at 13-13.2%, whereas some who picked later hit as high as 17%.

In 1983, the reds were marred by grapevine worms and rot.

But bizarrely, the reds are going to be totally different in style to the whites this year. There was much less shatter in red and thus the clusters are very compact and plump this year. Skins are thin. For bad farmers, we could end up with something a bit like 2011 but more dilute. Whereas the good farmers will be in a very different place, as I said before, maybe a bit like a 1993-2002-2017.

posts.name: Erlend @ntonsen
posts.cooked: Any updates?

posts.name: Edvards Antonovs
posts.cooked: Seems like a nasty heatwave is hitting burgundy so it would be interesting to know if anyone is rescheduling their harvest ASAP, especially the reds. Or maybe this final heat is perfect to let reds properly ripen given that it was quite a cool summer all in all?

posts.name: bruce curfman
posts.cooked:
We arrived in Puligny yesterday evening, and yes we are in the middle of an extended heat wave that is expected to last through the week, at least. Harvest is in full swing, hurry-up mode! I’ve rarely seen such cloudless skies and heat for an extended period this time of year. There does appear to be some relief coming next week with cooler temps and possibly rain.
Not sure how the harvest is shaking out yet, red vs white. Of course we’re in Puligny so seeing white coming in. We will take a driving tour today to see more what’s going on around the Côte.

posts.name: bruce curfman
posts.cooked: William, did you get your Beaune 1er parcel picked yet? How is it looking?

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked: Some news: from what I heard, in red, Claude Dugat started two days ago (4 Sept). Always one of the first to start for pinot noir. Thomas Bouley was harvesting today as well (maybe he started before, not sure). Another estate I heard of will only start on 14 Sept.

posts.name: Nikolaj Lehto
posts.cooked: Seeing on Instagram that a lot of producers have picked whites in Puligny/Chassagne/Meursault these past few days. Including 1er and GC.

posts.name: bruce curfman
posts.cooked:
Stopped by Montrachet on the way back from lunch, Laguiche still picking @1:00 pm. I’m assuming they had started sometime earlier in the morning, and perhaps nearly done. The grapes being loaded in trays 6-8 inches deep to keep them from pressing themselves as in larger bins like most surrounding vineyard harvesting . The grapes looked plump and juicy, even glistening with a little juice leaking in the mid day sun.

A few other premiere Cru vineyards, part of Pucelles and Caillerets (not sure of producers) seem to be finishing up. Not surprisingly, later in the hottest part of the day, harvesting activity subsided to a standstill. I was told that most Grand and Premiere Crus (whites) were essentially done harvesting. Also, all I heard about reds was that “they were more complicated”. I’ll wait for William’s take on that!

posts.name: Kent Comley
posts.cooked:
I don’t like the sound of harvesting Chardonnay in the heat of the day.
Perhaps they need to let loose the mechanical harvesters in Montrachet

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: A nasty heatwave would be a bit of an exaggeration: highs in the low 30s centigrade. But it does make picking in the afternoon rather less desirable. I would have preferred cooler temperatures at this stage, but it is certainly preferable to rain and the consequent rot.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Not even remotely.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
This warm, sunny weather is really saving (at least up to a point) anyone who had overcropped their reds and was liable to have to pick them unripe to avoid picking them rotten. It should make for a more consistent red vintage that I was anticipating.

The most obvious parallel is increasingly looking like 1999.

posts.name: Mike_M3y3rs
posts.cooked: I can’t add any expertise to what William reports. But can say that I saw a lot picking in Montrachet/Batard Montrachet yesterday. Was told DRC was picking there this morning. Walked up above Vosnee this morning and crews were picking lower portions of Les Petit Monts and Aux Champs Perdrix - not sure for who though. Looked to me like La Romanee had already been picked. And lots of processing all day at Comte Liger Belair.
